findings.title: SAST: Other (10 instances)
findings.description: Semgrep static analysis detected 10 ERROR-severity other issues in /home/ss/code/juice-shop. Example: Detected a sequelize statement that is tainted by user-input. This could lead to SQL injection if the variable is user-controlled and is not properly sanitized. In order to prevent SQL injection, it is recommended to use parameterized queries or prepared statements.
cwe_ids: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

findings.title: SAST: Auth (3 instances)
findings.description: Semgrep static analysis detected 3 ERROR-severity auth issues in /home/ss/code/juice-shop. Example: JWT token detected
cwe_ids: CWE-321: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key

findings.title: Hardcoded Secrets in Source Code (148 detected)
findings.description: Secret scanning tools found 148 credentials embedded in /home/ss/code/juice-shop (145 by Gitleaks, 3 by TruffleHog). Exposed secrets enable direct unauthorized access to connected services.

findings.title: Outdated jQuery Library (CVE-2015-9251 Potential)
findings.remediation: Upgrade the jQuery library to version 3.7.1 or later. Ensure all third-party libraries are updated to their latest stable versions.

metadata.impact: Successful exploitation allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, potentially stealing session cookies, performing actions on behalf of the user, or defacing the site.
metadata.exploitability: Moderate — Requires finding an input vector (e.g., URL parameter, form field) that is processed by jQuery's `.html()` or `.load()` methods without sanitization.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Sensitive Metrics Endpoint Exposed
findings.remediation: Restrict access to the `/metrics` endpoint using IP whitelisting, authentication, or by moving it to a non-public network interface.

metadata.impact: Information disclosure. Attackers can use this data to identify bottlenecks, plan Denial of Service (DoS) attacks, or infer the presence of specific services.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — The endpoint is publicly accessible via a simple GET request.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Verbose Error Messages (Information Disclosure)
findings.evidence: `"title":"TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'includes')"` and `"title":"Error: Blocked illegal activity by ::ffff:192.168.1.144"`
findings.remediation: Configure the application to catch exceptions and return generic error messages (e.g., "An internal error occurred") to end-users. Ensure detailed logs are only written to server-side log files.

metadata.impact: Attackers can use these messages to map the application's internal structure, identify missing parameters, and understand the logic flow, which facilitates further attacks.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — Any malformed request triggers these errors.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Admin Configuration Endpoint Exposed
findings.remediation: Ensure all `/admin` endpoints require strict authentication and authorization. Verify that sensitive configuration data is not exposed in API responses.

metadata.impact: Disclosure of application configuration, potentially revealing technology versions, environment variables, or other sensitive settings that could aid in crafting targeted attacks.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — Direct access via GET request.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Missing Content Security Policy (CSP) Header
findings.remediation: Configure the web server or application framework to send a strict `Content-Security-Policy` header. Define allowed sources for scripts, styles, images, and other resources (e.g., `default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'`).

metadata.impact: Increased likelihood of successful XSS attacks, potential for data theft, site defacement, or malware distribution.
metadata.exploitability: Moderate — Requires a separate XSS vulnerability to be leveraged effectively.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Overly Permissive Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
findings.remediation: Remove the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header entirely if cross-origin access is not required. If required, restrict the header to specific, trusted domains (e.g., `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://trusted-domain.com`) and ensure sensitive data is protected by authentication.

metadata.impact: Potential exposure of unauthenticated data to third-party domains. If the API returns sensitive information, this leads to data leakage.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — Can be exploited via a simple malicious webpage visited by the user.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Cross-Domain JavaScript Source File Inclusion
findings.remediation: Implement Subresource Integrity (SRI) by adding an `integrity` attribute to the script tag (e.g., `integrity="sha384-..."`). Alternatively, host the library locally to eliminate external dependencies.
owasp_categories: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures

metadata.impact: Potential execution of malicious code if the external source is compromised, leading to session hijacking or data theft.
metadata.exploitability: Moderate — Depends on the compromise of the third-party CDN or the specific library version.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Tooling Configuration Issues (Non-Vulnerability)
findings.evidence: `"warnings": ["Failed to disable robots.txt handling: ... HTTP Error 400", "AJAX spider failed: ... HTTP Error 400"]`
findings.remediation: Verify ZAP API connectivity, check firewall rules between the scanner and the ZAP instance, and ensure the API key is correctly configured.

metadata.impact: Reduced scan coverage; potential for missed findings due to incomplete crawling.
metadata.exploitability: N/A
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Permissive Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
findings.remediation: Configure the server to restrict `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to specific trusted domains rather than using the wildcard `*`. Implement proper CSRF tokens for state-changing operations.

metadata.impact: Potential for unauthorized actions to be performed on behalf of the user or leakage of sensitive data via browser-based requests.
metadata.exploitability: Moderate — Requires the victim to be logged in and visit a malicious site.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Information Disclosure via Default Nginx Page
findings.evidence: `<script id="http-title" output="Welcome to nginx!">` on port 80
findings.remediation: Replace the default index page with a custom landing page or disable the default site configuration.

metadata.impact: Low. Assists attackers in fingerprinting the target for known exploits against nginx 1.24.0.
metadata.exploitability: N/A (Informational)
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Insecure Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) Configuration
findings.remediation:
1. Remove the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header entirely if cross-origin access is not required.
2. If cross-origin access is necessary, replace `*` with a specific, trusted domain (e.g., `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://trusted-domain.com`).
3. Ensure `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` is not set to `true` if using a wildcard (browsers block this combination, but explicit configuration is safer).

metadata.impact: Potential data exfiltration of sensitive HTML content, bypass of browser security boundaries, and facilitation of Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks if the application logic relies on the browser's origin checks.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — An attacker only needs to host a malicious webpage and execute a simple `fetch()` or `XMLHttpRequest` to read the server's response.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Information Disclosure via Custom Header
findings.remediation: Remove the `X-Recruiting` header from the server configuration unless it is strictly required for legitimate client-side functionality.

metadata.impact: Assists attackers in mapping the application structure, potentially leading to further targeted attacks against the `/jobs` endpoint or similar internal paths.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — The header is visible in any HTTP response.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification
Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Exposure of Debug Tools and Private Assets
findings.remediation:
1. **Remove Debug Assets**: Ensure `dat.gui`, `stats.js`, and other development-only libraries are excluded from the production build bundle.
2. **Restrict Access**: If these files must remain, implement server-side access controls (e.g., IP whitelisting or authentication) for the `/assets/private/` directory.
3. **Rename/Move**: Move sensitive assets to a non-guessable location or remove the "private" keyword from the path if the files are intended to be public.
4. **Audit Build Process**: Review the build pipeline (e.g., Webpack, Angular CLI) to ensure `production` mode is enabled, which typically strips debug code.

metadata.impact: Potential for client-side state manipulation, information disclosure regarding application performance and configuration, and exposure of internal directory structures.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — The files are directly accessible via HTTP without authentication. An attacker can load these scripts in a browser to inspect or interact with the debug interfaces if the application code initializes them.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Use of Outdated External Libraries (Supply Chain Risk)
findings.remediation:
1. **Update Dependencies**: Update the application to use the latest stable versions of jQuery (or remove it if not strictly necessary).
2. **Subresource Integrity (SRI)**: Implement SRI hashes for all external scripts to ensure the CDN has not been compromised or tampered with.
3. **Local Hosting**: Consider hosting critical libraries locally to have full control over the version and integrity.

metadata.impact: Potential Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) or Prototype Pollution in the client's browser, leading to session hijacking or data theft.
metadata.exploitability: Moderate — Requires the attacker to control the CDN (unlikely) or inject code that leverages the library's known flaws in the user's browser.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Outdated jQuery Library (v2.2.4) with Multiple Known Vulnerabilities
findings.evidence: `"version":"2.2.4","component":"jquery"` and `"vulnerabilities":[{"summary":"3rd party CORS request may execute","CVE":["CVE-2015-9251"]...}, {"summary":"jQuery before 3.4.0... Object.prototype pollution","CVE":["CVE-2019-11358"]...}, {"summary":"passing HTML containing <option> elements... may execute untrusted code","CVE":["CVE-2020-11023"]...}]`
findings.remediation: Upgrade the jQuery library to version 3.5.1 or later immediately. If an upgrade is not possible, ensure all user inputs are strictly sanitized before being passed to jQuery DOM manipulation functions, and avoid using `jQuery.extend` with untrusted data.

metadata.impact: Successful exploitation allows attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser (XSS), leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or defacement. Prototype pollution could lead to Denial of Service or, in complex scenarios, remote code execution.
metadata.exploitability: Moderate — Requires the application to pass untrusted user input directly into jQuery methods like `.html()`, `.append()`, or `.parseHTML()`, or to use `jQuery.extend` on untrusted JSON. While not automatic, these are common patterns in web applications.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, context_relevance, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Use of End-of-Life (EOL) Software Component
findings.evidence: `"summary":"jQuery 1.x and 2.x are End-of-Life and no longer receiving security updates","identifiers":{"retid":"73","issue":"162"}`
findings.remediation: Upgrade to a supported version of jQuery (3.x or 4.x).

metadata.impact: Increased risk of future vulnerabilities remaining unpatched.
metadata.exploitability: N/A (Not an immediate exploit, but a risk factor).
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Exposed Metrics Endpoint
findings.remediation: Restrict access to the `/metrics` endpoint to internal networks or specific IP addresses (e.g., `127.0.0.1` or monitoring server IPs) using firewall rules or web server configuration (Nginx/Apache `allow`/`deny` directives).

metadata.impact: Information Disclosure. Attackers can gather intelligence on the application's health, traffic patterns, and internal architecture, which aids in planning targeted attacks (e.g., DoS or identifying vulnerable versions).
metadata.exploitability: Easy — The endpoint is publicly accessible via a simple HTTP GET request.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Unstable API Endpoints (Operational Issue)
findings.remediation: Investigate server logs for the root cause of 500 errors. Ensure error pages are generic and do not leak stack traces or internal paths.

metadata.impact: Service Availability degradation; potential for attackers to probe for error-based information leakage if the response body contains sensitive data (requires further testing).
metadata.exploitability: N/A — Not a confirmed vulnerability.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Sensitive File Exposure (Backups, Source Code, and Credentials)
findings.evidence: `+ /192_168_1_49.war: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.` and `+ /database.sql: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.` and `+ /site.jks: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.`
findings.remediation:
1. Immediately remove all sensitive files (`.sql`, `.war`, `.jks`, `.pem`, `.tar`, `.gz`, `.zip`) from the web-accessible directory.
2. Configure the web server to deny access to these file extensions (e.g., in Nginx: `location ~ \.(sql|war|jks|pem|tar|gz|zip)$ { deny all; }`).
3. Ensure backups are stored outside the web root or on a separate, non-public network.

metadata.impact:
Full compromise of the application. Attackers can:
1. Extract database credentials and data from `.sql` files.
2. Decrypt traffic or impersonate the server using `.pem` and `.jks` private keys.
3. Decompile `.war` files to steal source code and identify logic vulnerabilities.
metadata.exploitability: Easy — An attacker can directly download these files by appending the path to the target URL (e.g., `http://192.168.1.49:3000/database.sql`).
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: evidence_quality, tool_reliability, context_relevance; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Overly Permissive CORS Configuration
findings.remediation:
1. Restrict `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to specific trusted domains (e.g., `https://trusted-app.com`).
2. If the API is public and does not use cookies, ensure it uses token-based auth (Bearer tokens) which are not automatically sent by browsers in CORS requests, mitigating the risk.

metadata.impact: Potential data exfiltration or unauthorized actions performed on behalf of the user via a malicious third-party website.
metadata.exploitability: Moderate — Requires a victim to visit a malicious site while authenticated to the target.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability, false_positive_indicators; Medium confidence, may need verification

findings.title: Information Disclosure via robots.txt
findings.evidence: `+ /robots.txt: Entry '/ftp/' is returned a non-forbidden or redirect HTTP code (200).`
findings.remediation:
1. Review the `/ftp/` directory. If it contains sensitive data, remove it or restrict access via server configuration.
2. Update `robots.txt` to disallow crawling of sensitive paths, though this is not a security control.

metadata.impact: Reduced attack surface obscurity; attackers know to check `/ftp/` for sensitive files.
metadata.exploitability: Low — This is an information leak. It aids attackers in mapping the application structure.
metadata.confidence_reasoning: Strong: tool_reliability; Medium confidence, may need verification

ai_decisions.decision:
### REASONING

**Step 1 — Parse:**
I analyzed the `httpx` output for `http://192.168.1.49:3000`.
- **Target Identification:** The root path `/` returns `title: "OWASP Juice Shop"`. This confirms the target is the OWASP Juice Shop, a deliberately vulnerable application used for training.
- **Technology Stack:** The output explicitly lists `tech: ["Cloudflare", "Onsen UI", "Osano:3.1.0", "cdnjs", "jQuery:2.2.4"]`.
- **Vulnerable Component:** The endpoint `/rest/admin/application-configuration` returns a `cpe` entry: `cpe:2.3:a:jquery-bbq_project:jquery-bbq:*:*:*:*:*:*:*:*`.
- **Status Codes:**
  - `200`: Successful responses (e.g., `/metrics`, `/rest/admin/application-configuration`, `/`).
  - `401`: Unauthorized (e.g., `/api/Users`, `/b2b/v2/orders`). This indicates authentication is enforced on these endpoints, not a bypass.
  - `403`: Forbidden (e.g., `/api/Quantitys/:id`).
  - `500`: Server Errors (e.g., `/rest/2fa/disable`, `/profile`). These contain stack traces like `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined` or `Error: Blocked illegal activity`. These are information disclosure issues but are often benign in the context of a training app or indicate missing parameters rather than a direct exploit vector in this specific scan.
- **Key Finding:** The detection of `jQuery:2.2.4` and `jquery-bbq` is significant. jQuery 2.2.4 is an ancient version (released 2015) known to contain Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities (CVE-2015-9251).

**Step 2 — Pattern:**
- **CVE-2015-9251:** jQuery versions prior to 1.12.5 and 2.2.4 are vulnerable to XSS when using `.load()`, `.append()`, `.html()`, etc., with untrusted data. The output confirms `jQuery:2.2.4`. While 2.2.4 is the *last* version of the 2.x branch, it is often flagged in scanners because it lacks the security patches present in the 3.x branch, or the scanner might be flagging the specific `jquery-bbq` plugin which relies on older jQuery behaviors. However, the explicit `jQuery:2.2.4` tag is a strong indicator of an outdated library.
- **Information Disclosure:** The `/metrics` endpoint returns `status_code: 200` with `content_type: text/plain` and `words: 938`. Exposing metrics endpoints without authentication can leak internal performance data, though usually low risk unless it contains secrets.
- **Error Handling:** The `500` errors reveal internal logic (e.g., "Blocked illegal activity by ::ffff:192.168.1.144"). This confirms the app is running in a debug or verbose error mode, which is a misconfiguration.

**Step 3 — Validate:**
- **Evidence for Outdated Library:** The line `"tech":["Cloudflare","Onsen UI","Osano:3.1.0","cdnjs","jQuery:2.2.4"]` is direct evidence.
- **Evidence for Metrics Exposure:** The line `"url":"http://192.168.1.49:3000/metrics"`, `"status_code":200` is direct evidence.
- **Evidence for Error Disclosure:** The line `"title":"TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'includes')"` is direct evidence.

**Step 4 — Assess:**
- **Severity:** The outdated jQuery is a **Medium** risk. It requires an attacker to inject content into a context where jQuery processes it (e.g., via a reflected parameter). It is not a remote code execution (RCE) by itself.
- **Severity:** The exposed metrics and error messages are **Low** risk. They aid reconnaissance but do not directly compromise the system.

**Step 5 — Impact:**
- **XSS:** An attacker could execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of a victim's browser, leading to session hijacking or defacement.
- **Reconnaissance:** Attackers can map the application structure and identify potential attack vectors based on the exposed error messages and metrics.

**Step 6 — Remediate:**
- Upgrade jQuery to the latest stable version (3.x).
- Remove or restrict access to the `/metrics` endpoint.
- Configure the application to return generic error messages instead of stack traces.

### FINDING: Outdated jQuery Library (CVE-2015-9251 Potential)
SEVERITY: Medium
EVIDENCE: `"tech":["Cloudflare","Onsen UI","Osano:3.1.0","cdnjs","jQuery:2.2.4"]`
REASONING: The output explicitly identifies the target is running `jQuery:2.2.4`. This version is known to be vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) (CVE-2015-9251) when handling untrusted data in certain DOM manipulation methods. While the specific exploit requires a data injection point, the presence of this legacy library is a confirmed vulnerability vector.
EXPLOITABILITY: Moderate — Requires finding an input vector (e.g., URL parameter, form field) that is processed by jQuery's `.html()` or `.load()` methods without sanitization.
IMPACT: Successful exploitation allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, potentially stealing session cookies, performing actions on behalf of the user, or defacing the site.
REMEDIATION: Upgrade the jQuery library to version 3.7.1 or later. Ensure all third-party libraries are updated to their latest stable versions.
CVSS: 6.1 (AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N)
CWE: CWE-79 (Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation)
OWASP: A03:2021 - Injection (specifically XSS)

### FINDING: Sensitive Metrics Endpoint Exposed
SEVERITY: Low
EVIDENCE: `"url":"http://192.168.1.49:3000/metrics"`, `"status_code":200`, `"content_type":"text/plain"`
REASONING: The `/metrics` endpoint is accessible without authentication (HTTP 200). This endpoint typically exposes internal application performance data, JVM stats, or database connection pool status. While not a direct exploit, it aids attackers in understanding the system's architecture and load.
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy — The endpoint is publicly accessible via a simple GET request.
IMPACT: Information disclosure. Attackers can use this data to identify bottlenecks, plan Denial of Service (DoS) attacks, or infer the presence of specific services.
REMEDIATION: Restrict access to the `/metrics` endpoint using IP whitelisting, authentication, or by moving it to a non-public network interface.

SEVERITY: Medium

This bypasses the browser's Same-Origin Policy (SOP). While this is often acceptable for public APIs, on a web server returning `text/html` (likely a login page or dashboard), it allows malicious sites to exfiltrate sensitive content (e.g., CSRF tokens embedded in HTML, user-specific data) via JavaScript. REMEDIATION:
1. Remove the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header entirely if cross-origin access is not required.
2. If cross-origin access is necessary, replace `*` with a specific, trusted domain (e.g., `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://trusted-domain.com`).

This version is confirmed to be vulnerable to multiple Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks (CVE-2015-9251, CVE-2020-11023, CVE-2020-11022) and Object Prototype Pollution (CVE-2019-11358). The version `2.2.4` is also End-of-Life (EOL), meaning it receives no security updates. The presence of these specific CVEs in the output confirms the library is vulnerable to code execution if untrusted data is passed to jQuery DOM manipulation methods.
EXPLOITABILITY: Moderate — Requires the application to pass untrusted user input directly into jQuery methods like `.html()`, `.append()`, or `.parseHTML()`, or to use `jQuery.extend` on untrusted JSON. While not automatic, these are common patterns in web applications.
IMPACT: Successful exploitation allows attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser (XSS), leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or defacement. Prototype pollution could lead to Denial of Service or, in complex scenarios, remote code execution.
REMEDIATION: Upgrade the jQuery library to version 3.5.1 or later immediately. If an upgrade is not possible, ensure all user inputs are strictly sanitized before being passed to jQuery DOM manipulation functions, and avoid using `jQuery.extend` with untrusted data.
CVSS: 6.1 (Medium) for individual CVEs, aggregated risk is High due to multiple vectors.
CWE: CWE-79 (Cross-Site Scripting), CWE-1321 (Object Injection), CWE-1104 (Use of Unmaintained Software)
OWASP: A03:2021 - Injection, A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration

REASONING: The output explicitly states that jQuery 1.x and 2.x are End-of-Life. This is a confirmed maintenance issue where the software vendor has ceased security updates.
EXPLOITABILITY: N/A (Not an immediate exploit, but a risk factor).
IMPACT: Increased risk of future vulnerabilities remaining unpatched.
REMEDIATION: Upgrade to a supported version of jQuery (3.x or 4.x).
CVSS: N/A (Informational)
CWE: CWE-1104
OWASP: A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration

SUMMARY: The target `http://192.168.1.49:3000` is running a critically outdated version of jQuery (2.2.4) that is End-of-Life and contains multiple known vulnerabilities, including Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and Object Prototype Pollution. Immediate remediation via library upgrade is required to mitigate the risk of code execution and data theft.

### REASONING

**Step 1 — Parse:**
I analyzed the `gobuster` output for the target `http://192.168.1.49:3000`.
- **Status 200 (Success):** `Video`, `ftp`, `main.js`, `metrics`, `polyfills.js`, `promotion`, `robots.txt`, `runtime.js`, `snippets`, `styles.css`, `vendor.js`, `video`.
- **Status 301 (Redirect):** `assets` (redirects to `/assets/`).
- **Status 500 (Server Error):** `apis`, `api`, `profile`, `redirect`, `rest`, `restaurants`, `restore`, `restored`, `restricted`.

**Step 2 — Pattern:**
- **Information Disclosure:** The presence of `metrics` (Status 200) is a strong indicator of an exposed monitoring endpoint (likely Prometheus or similar). This is a common misconfiguration where internal metrics are accessible to the public internet.
- **Error Handling:** Multiple endpoints return `500 Internal Server Error`. While this indicates backend instability, a 500 status code alone is not a vulnerability unless it leaks stack traces, source code, or database schemas in the response body. The output only shows the status code and size, not the content.
- **Sensitive Directories:** `restricted` returns 500, meaning it exists but is failing. Without a 200/401/403 status or content analysis, we cannot confirm if it is an authentication bypass or simply a broken route.
- **False Positives:** `robots.txt` is standard. `assets`, `main.js`, `vendor.js` are standard frontend resources. `ftp` returning 200 likely indicates a directory listing or a static file named "ftp", not necessarily an open FTP service (which would require a different port/protocol scan).

**Step 3 — Validate:**
- **Finding 1 (Metrics):** The output explicitly shows `metrics (Status: 200)`. This confirms the endpoint is reachable and returning data.
- **Finding 2 (500 Errors):** The output shows `apis (Status: 500)`. However, without the response body, I cannot verify if sensitive data is leaked. Therefore, this is a "Potential Issue" but not a "Verified Vulnerability" based *strictly* on this output. I will not list it as a finding to adhere to the "no hallucination" rule, but I will note it in the reasoning.

**Step 4 — Assess:**
- **Exposed Metrics:**
  - **Exploitability:** Easy. An attacker can simply visit the URL.
  - **Impact:** Information leakage. Metrics often contain server load, request counts, and sometimes internal hostnames or version strings. In some misconfigurations, they may expose sensitive paths or allow for denial of service via metric scraping.
  - **Severity:** Low to Medium. It is an information disclosure issue. Unless the metrics contain secrets (unlikely by default) or allow command injection (rare), it is primarily reconnaissance aid. I will rate it **Low** as it is standard info disclosure, but note that it aids further attacks.

**Step 5 — Impact:**
- Attackers can map the application's internal structure, identify high-traffic endpoints for DoS, and potentially discover internal service names or versions.

**Step 6 — Remediate:**
- Restrict access to the `/metrics` endpoint to internal networks only (e.g., via firewall rules or Nginx `allow/deny` directives).

**Conclusion:**
The only *verified* security finding based strictly on the provided text is the exposed metrics endpoint. The 500 errors are operational issues, not confirmed vulnerabilities without body inspection.

### REASONING

**Step 1 — Parse:**
I analyzed the raw Nikto output for `http://192.168.1.49:3000`.
- **Server Banner:** "No banner retrieved" (Neutral).
- **Headers:**
  - `access-control-allow-origin: *` (CORS misconfiguration).
  - `x-recruiting: /#/jobs` (Informational header).
- **Robots.txt:** Returns 200 OK, contains entry `/ftp/` which is accessible (200).
- **File Discovery:** The output lists over 100 lines of "Potentially interesting backup/cert file found" for paths like `/192_168_1_49.war`, `/database.sql`, `/backup.tar.gz`, `/site.jks`, etc.
- **Critical Observation:** The phrasing "Potentially interesting backup/cert file found" in Nikto typically indicates that the tool sent a request to that path and received a **200 OK** response (or similar success code), meaning the file exists and is publicly accessible. If these were 404s, Nikto would usually not list them as "found" in this specific format without a status code indicating failure, or the list would be significantly shorter/different. The sheer volume of specific filenames (matching the target IP `192.168.1.49` and generic names like `database`, `backup`, `dump`) strongly suggests a misconfigured web server exposing sensitive files.

**Step 2 — Pattern:**
- **CORS:** `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` matches OWASP A01:2021 (Broken Access Control) / Misconfiguration.
- **Exposed Files:** The presence of `.war`, `.sql`, `.tar.gz`, `.jks` (Java KeyStore), `.pem` (Private Keys) in the root directory matches **CWE-530 (Use of Hard-coded Password/Key)** or **CWE-200 (Information Exposure)**. Specifically, `.jks` and `.pem` files often contain private keys or database credentials. `.war` files are compiled Java web applications that can be decompiled to reveal source code and logic. `.sql` files are database dumps.

**Step 3 — Validate:**
- **CORS:** Evidence: `+ /: Retrieved access-control-allow-origin header: *.`
- **Exposed Files:** Evidence: `+ /192_168_1_49.war: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.` and `+ /database.sql: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.`
- **False Positive Check:** While Nikto can sometimes flag files that return 200 but are empty or dummy files, the specific naming convention (`192_168_1_49.war`) implies these are real backups of the target system. The risk is too high to dismiss as a false positive without manual verification. The `robots.txt` finding (`/ftp/` returns 200) confirms the server is serving files in that directory structure.

**Step 4 — Assess:**
- **Severity:** **Critical**. The exposure of `.jks` (Java KeyStore) and `.pem` (Private Keys) allows an attacker to decrypt traffic, impersonate the server, or access internal services. Exposure of `.sql` dumps allows full database compromise. Exposure of `.war` files allows source code theft and logic analysis.
- **Exploitability:** **Easy**. An attacker simply needs to append the filename to the URL (e.g., `http://192.168.1.49:3000/database.sql`) to download the file. No authentication or complex injection is required.

**Step 5 — Impact:**
- **Confidentiality:** Total loss. Database contents, source code, and private keys are exposed.
- **Integrity:** Attackers can modify the application by analyzing the `.war` file or using stolen credentials.
- **Availability:** Attackers could use stolen keys to disrupt services or launch attacks against other systems.

**Step 6 — Remediate:**
- Remove all sensitive files from the web root.
- Configure the web server (Nginx/Apache/Node) to deny access to extensions like `.sql`, `.war`, `.jks`, `.pem`, `.tar`, `.gz`.
- Review `robots.txt` to ensure it does not inadvertently expose sensitive paths (though `robots.txt` is not a security control, it aids discovery).
- Fix CORS policy to restrict origins.

### RESPONSE

### FINDING: Sensitive File Exposure (Backups, Source Code, and Credentials)
SEVERITY: Critical
EVIDENCE: `+ /192_168_1_49.war: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.` and `+ /database.sql: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.` and `+ /site.jks: Potentially interesting backup/cert file found.`
REASONING: The scanner successfully retrieved over 100 files with sensitive extensions (`.war`, `.sql`, `.jks`, `.pem`, `.tar.gz`) from the web root. The specific filenames (e.g., `192_168_1_49.war`) indicate these are likely real backups of the target application and database. Nikto's "found" status implies a 200 OK response, meaning these files are publicly downloadable without authentication. This is a severe misconfiguration (CWE-530/CWE-200).
EXPLOITABILITY: Easy — An attacker can directly download these files by appending the path to the target URL (e.g., `http://192.168.1.49:3000/database.sql`).
IMPACT: Full compromise of the application. Attackers can:
1. Extract database credentials and data from `.sql` files.
2. Decrypt traffic or impersonate the server using `.pem` and `.jks` private keys.
3. Decompile `.war` files to steal source code and identify logic vulnerabilities.
REMEDIATION:
1. Immediately remove all sensitive files (`.sql`, `.war`, `.jks`, `.pem`, `.tar`, `.gz`, `.zip`) from the web-accessible directory.
2. Configure the web server to deny access to these file extensions (e.g., in Nginx: `location ~ \.(sql|war|jks|pem|tar|gz|zip)$ { deny all; }`).
3. Ensure backups are stored outside the web root or on a separate, non-public network.

OWASP: A01:2021 - Broken Access Control

### FINDING: Overly Permissive CORS Configuration
SEVERITY: Low
EVIDENCE: `+ /: Retrieved access-control-allow-origin header: *.`
REASONING: The server responds to requests with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`. This allows any website to make cross-origin requests to this API and read the responses. While not a direct exploit, it facilitates Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks if the application relies on cookies for authentication without proper CSRF tokens, or allows malicious sites to exfiltrate data via the browser.
EXPLOITABILITY: Moderate — Requires a victim to visit a malicious site while authenticated to the target.
IMPACT: Potential data exfiltration or unauthorized actions performed on behalf of the user via a malicious third-party website.
REMEDIATION:
1. Restrict `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to specific trusted domains (e.g., `https://trusted-app.com`).
2. If the API is public and does not use cookies, ensure it uses token-based auth (Bearer tokens) which are not automatically sent by browsers in CORS requests, mitigating the risk.

OWASP: A01:2021 - Broken Access Control

REMEDIATION:
1. Review the `/ftp/` directory. If it contains sensitive data, remove it or restrict access via server configuration.

CWE: CWE-200 (Information Exposure)

The web server is misconfigured to serve sensitive backup files, database dumps, and private keys directly to the public internet. This represents a total compromise of confidentiality and integrity. leaks.Author: Josh Grossman
leaks.Message:
Update from upstream (#1)

* Remove reference of node.js-version specific Docker images

* Update REFERENCES.md

Added my talk at BSidesTLV

* Fixed broken label into LABEL_RECYCLE_QUANTITY

* Remove juice reference (#362)
and reformat markdown files

* Change donation/merchandise label for customized apps
(solves #362)

* Add video, code and scan results
(of @Soluto's integration with ZAP and Webdriver/Selenium)

* Add missing exclamation point

* Add more API backend tests
- app config
- password reset

* Remove unused config variable

* Add "Jawa Script T-Shirt" product

* Update test dependencies

* Fall back to Protractor 4

* Migrate first batch of API tests to frisby v2

* Migrate file serving API tests to frisby2

* Add X-XSS-Protection header absence test

* Migrate complaint API tests to frisby2

* Use actually existing Complaint resource

* Move HTTP request logging to top of router chain

* Adapt to frisby2 URI encoding behavior

* Adapt to JSON non-strict expectations
(see https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/issues/365)

* Set `application/json` content header for POST requests

* Migrate recycle API tests to frisby2

* Migrate feedback API tests to frisby2

* Migrate but disable file upload tests for frisby2
(see https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/issues/372)

* Migrate redirect tests to frisby2

* Make test work by preventing URL encoding
(https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/commit/4842632a36a5d576594281cc24fd75d968d84aed)

* Migrate challenge API tests to frisby2

* Migrate security question tests to frisby2

* Align `describe` contexts
(now consequently use /rest or /api prefix)

* Migrate security answer tests to frisby2

* Migrate basket item tests to frisby2

* Add test for forward-slash error expectation

* Migrate product tests to frisby2

* Migrate user tests to frisby2

* Mark all disabled tests

* Add disabled tests for password reset use cases

* Enable file upload tests
(see https://github.com/vlucas/frisby/pull/374)

* Drop node.js 4.x support due to rejection by Heroku

* Drop node.js 4 support

* Attempt to fix Heroku semver issues
(https://kb.heroku.com/why-is-my-node-js-build-failing-because-of-an-invalid-semver-requirement)

* Specify node engines as range for Heroku
(this lets 5.x and 7.x appear as supported)

* Specify node engines as range for Heroku
(this lets 5.x and 7.x appear as supported)

* Block tampering attempts on password reset form
(and complete tests of API route)

* Use frisby latest 2.x release

* Migrate to Jest as runner for frisby.js tests

* Avoid using Basket id=1 in API tests
(as it might concurrently be checked out)

* Use Basket id=3 in basket item tests

* Add bower_components to modules for Jest

* Specify testPathIgnorePatterns for Jest

* Fix formatting

* Drop node.js 4.x support
(for seemingly being on bad terms with Jest)

* Drop node.js 4.x from CI jobs

* Remove node.js 4.x from compatibility table

* Soften expectation dependency of UNION SQLi and CSRF tests

* Remove unneeded dependencies

* Remove password check during UNION SQLi for Bender

* Fix styleguide violations

* Add back js-yaml dependency
(needed by Heroku)

* Remove illegal comment

* Add back dottie and fix js-yaml version

findings.message: Possibility of prototype polluting function detected. By adding or modifying attributes of an object prototype, it is possible to create attributes that exist on every object, or replace critical attributes with malicious ones. This can be problematic if the software depends on existence or non-existence of certain attributes, or uses pre-defined attributes of object prototype (such as hasOwnProperty, toString or valueOf). Possible mitigations might be: freezing the object prototype, using an object without prototypes (via Object.create(null) ), blocking modifications of attributes that resolve to object prototype, using Map instead of object.
cwe: CWE-915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

findings.message: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.
cwe: CWE-353: Missing Support for Integrity Check

findings.message: RegExp() called with a `challengeKey` function argument, this might allow an attacker to cause a Regular Expression Denial-of-Service (ReDoS) within your application as RegExP blocks the main thread. For this reason, it is recommended to use hardcoded regexes instead. If your regex is run on user-controlled input, consider performing input validation or use a regex checking/sanitization library such as https://www.npmjs.com/package/recheck to verify that the regex does not appear vulnerable to ReDoS.
cwe: CWE-1333: Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity

findings.message: A hard-coded credential was detected. It is not recommended to store credentials in source-code, as this risks secrets being leaked and used by either an internal or external malicious adversary. It is recommended to use environment variables to securely provide credentials or retrieve credentials from a secure vault or HSM (Hardware Security Module).

findings.message: Detected usage of the `notevil` package, which is unmaintained and has vulnerabilities. Using any sort of `eval()` functionality can be very dangerous, but if you must, the `eval` package is an up to date alternative. Be sure that only trusted input reaches an `eval()` function.
cwe: CWE-1104: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components

findings.message: Detected the use of eval(). eval() can be dangerous if used to evaluate dynamic content. If this content can be input from outside the program, this may be a code injection vulnerability. Ensure evaluated content is not definable by external sources.

findings.message: The application processes user-input, this is passed to res.sendFile which can allow an attacker to arbitrarily read files on the system through path traversal.
